question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: Short summary of the problem - External users see “502 – Bad Gateway” while logins from the internal network work fine. The same FQDN fails but a direct (alternate) path to the reverse proxy behaves differently (works)
context: SOURCE 15: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF40: Customer Portal – Login via external URL not possible Ticket number: TI-1594408 Company: Customer Company 40 AG Contact person: Contact Person 19 Owner: Employee 29 Origin: Phone Type: Incident Created on: 03.06.25 07:52 Closure Closure date: 18.06.25 15:33 Closure text: Analysis completed. Configuration adjusted according to architectural specifications. Function restored. Activities 03.06.25 07:45 Phone report. Issue: External users can no longer log in to the customer portal. URL: https://portal.kf40.ch Browser error message: 502 – Bad Gateway Internally (via internal network) login works. Priority: High (customers affected) 03.06.25 07:52 Ticket created Assigned to: Employee 29 Status: In Progress Notes 03.06.25 08:30 Helpdesk checks: • External DNS resolution correct • Certificate valid • HTTPS reachable • Internal backend (app server) reachable Test: External → 502 Internal → works Suspicion: Reverse Proxy / WAF 03.06.25 09:10 Customer adds: A new certificate was installed over the weekend. Additionally, there was a firewall update (security patch). 03.06.25 10:25 Helpdesk checks firewall: • Rule for port 443 present • NAT correct • No blocking visible in logs Load balancer status: Backend server marked as “Healthy”. 03.06.25 11:40 Test with direct IP to Reverse Proxy → works. Test via FQDN → 502 Notable: Header “X-Forwarded-Proto” missing in external request. 04.06.25 09:05 Customer asks whether certificate may be incorrectly bound. Certificate validation: • CN correct • SAN correct • Chain complete No obvious errors. 05.06.25 08:50 Ticket forwarded to Network / Security Team. Assigned group changed to: BU Network & Security 06.06.25 09:30 Taken over by System Engineer 21 WAF analysis: • Policy active • TLS inspection active • HTTP/2 enabled Notable: After patch, default profile was reloaded. 06.06.25 11:15 Test: TLS inspection temporarily disabled → Login works. Inspection re-enabled → 502 again. 09.06.25 10:05 Further analysis: App server expects header: X-Forwarded-Proto: https When TLS inspection is active, header is not forwarded. 09.06.25 14:40 Comparison with architecture documentation performed. Reference in internal document: “Blueprint: Secure Web Publishing – KF40 – Version 3.2” Section 4.3 describes special header forwarding in case of TLS offloading. 10.06.25 08:25 Blueprint document loaded from DMS. Relevant notes: • TLS termination must occur on WAF • Backend expects HTTPS signaling • Custom header rewrite rule required • Inspection policy must not remove HTTP headers • Specific exception defined for portal application 10.06.25 11:45 Configuration comparison with blueprint shows: • Custom header rewrite rule missing • HTTP profile does not match documented version • Inspection template “Default-Strict” active instead of “Portal-App-Template” 12.06.25 Internal coordination: Firewall patch overwrote template. Custom policy was not reassigned. 16.06.25 Configuration reviewed and adjusted according to blueprint. No technical details documented in ticket (security policy). 18.06.25 14:55 External access test successful. Multiple login attempts tested. Monitoring stable for 24 hours. Ticket closure 18.06.25 15:33 Dear Contact Person 19 The root cause was a deviation in the Reverse Proxy configuration from the documented architectural specification following a security patch. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal blueprint documentation. The customer portal is now fully accessible again. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: . - Tests showed the header X-Forwarded-Proto was missing on external requests. When TLS inspection on the WAF was temporarily disabled, external logins worked; re-enabling inspection produced 502 again

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: . - After a security/firewall patch the WAF/reverse-proxy configuration (inspection template and custom header rewrite rule) had been reset/overwritten which caused the backend to no longer see HTTPS semantics it expects

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company SOURCE 17: ﻿ general info & description This document describes the blueprint for the secure publication of web-based applications of Customer Company 40 over the Internet. The architecture is based on a Reverse Proxy and Web Application Firewall (WAF) concept with TLS termination at the perimeter level. The objective is to protect internal application servers from direct Internet exposure while ensuring secure and controlled communication with external users. The Web Publishing architecture is used for the following applications: • Customer portal (portal.kf40.ch) • Partner access • Internal web services with external interface Central principles: • No direct publication of internal servers • TLS termination exclusively on the WAF • Backend servers are located in a separate server VLAN • HTTPS semantics must be preserved for the backend • Standardized templates for all portal applications Best practices: • Use of dedicated WAF templates per application • Documented header rewrite rules • No use of generic “Default-Strict” profiles for productive portals • Validation against this blueprint after every security patch MUST BE OBSERVED: All changes to WAF profiles, TLS policies, or Reverse Proxy rules must be validated against this document. design The architecture consists of three logical zones: Internet → Perimeter Zone (WAF / Reverse Proxy) → Internal Application Zone Components 1. Web Application Firewall (WAF) o TLS termination o Inspection policy o Header rewrite o Load balancing 2. Reverse Proxy Layer o Forwarding to application servers o Host header handling o Session persistence 3. Backend Application Server o Expects HTTPS semantics o No direct Internet access o Internal authentication TLS Offloading TLS is terminated on the WAF. Communication between the WAF and backend is performed over HTTP by default. In order for the backend to correctly interpret the original HTTPS request, the following headers must be set: • X-Forwarded-Proto: https • X-Forwarded-Host These headers must not be removed or overwritten by inspection or security profiles. WAF Template For portal applications, the template: Portal-App-Template must be used. This template contains: • Header rewrite rules • HTTPS semantics preservation • HTTP/2 compatibility configuration • Session handling • Adjustments for authentication flows The use of other templates (e.g., Default-Strict) is not permitted for productive portal services. After Security Patches After firmware or security updates, it is mandatory to verify: • Whether the correct template is still active • Whether custom rewrite rules remain in place • Whether inspection policies have not been reset • Whether TLS profiles remain unchanged decision tree & use cases 1. Question: Is the application reachable internally but not externally? Yes → Proceed to question 2. No → The issue is likely not within the Web Publishing layer. 2. Question: Is a 502 or 503 error displayed during external access? Yes → Check WAF template and header rewrite rules. No → Proceed to question 3. 3. Question: Is there a redirect loop between HTTP and HTTPS? Yes → Check X-Forwarded-Proto header. No → Proceed to question 4. 4. Question: Was a certificate recently renewed or a security patch applied? Yes → Validate configuration according to section “After Security Patches”. No → Proceed to question 5. 5. Question: Is TLS inspection enabled? Yes → Verify that the Portal-App-Template is used. No → Check backend health status.

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: . What is the likely root cause - The most likely root cause is a change to the WAF / reverse-proxy configuration after a security patch that removed or reverted the custom header-rewrite rules and replaced the portal-specific template with a generic template. That caused TLS inspection to strip or fail to forward the X-Forwarded-Proto header, so the backend (which expects HTTPS signalling) returned 502 for external requests via the FQDN

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: . Should this be handled by 1st-level or 2nd-level support? - This is a 2nd-level (Network / Security / WAF / Reverse Proxy) issue. This template contains: • Header rewrite rules • HTTPS semantics preservation • HTTP/2 compatibility configuration • Session handling • Adjustments for authentication flows The use of other templates (e.g., Default-Strict) is not permitted for productive portal services. After Security Patches After firmware or security updates, it is mandatory to verify: • Whether the correct template is still active • Whether custom rewrite rules remain in place • Whether inspection policies have not been reset • Whether TLS profiles remain unchanged decision tree & use cases 1. Question: Is the application reachable internally but not externally? Yes → Proceed to question 2. No → The issue is likely not within the Web Publishing layer. 2. Question: Is a 502 or 503 error displayed during external access? Yes → Check WAF template and header rewrite rules. No → Proceed to question 3. 3. Question: Is there a redirect loop between HTTP and HTTPS? Yes → Check X-Forwarded-Proto header. No → Proceed to question 4. 4. Question: Was a certificate recently renewed or a security patch applied? Yes → Validate configuration according to section “After Security Patches”. No → Proceed to question 5. 5. Question: Is TLS inspection enabled? Yes → Verify that the Portal-App-Template is used. No → Check backend health status.

context: SOURCE 15: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF40: Customer Portal – Login via external URL not possible Ticket number: TI-1594408 Company: Customer Company 40 AG Contact person: Contact Person 19 Owner: Employee 29 Origin: Phone Type: Incident Created on: 03.06.25 07:52 Closure Closure date: 18.06.25 15:33 Closure text: Analysis completed. Configuration adjusted according to architectural specifications. Function restored. Activities 03.06.25 07:45 Phone report. Issue: External users can no longer log in to the customer portal. URL: https://portal.kf40.ch Browser error message: 502 – Bad Gateway Internally (via internal network) login works. Priority: High (customers affected) 03.06.25 07:52 Ticket created Assigned to: Employee 29 Status: In Progress Notes 03.06.25 08:30 Helpdesk checks: • External DNS resolution correct • Certificate valid • HTTPS reachable • Internal backend (app server) reachable Test: External → 502 Internal → works Suspicion: Reverse Proxy / WAF 03.06.25 09:10 Customer adds: A new certificate was installed over the weekend. Additionally, there was a firewall update (security patch). 03.06.25 10:25 Helpdesk checks firewall: • Rule for port 443 present • NAT correct • No blocking visible in logs Load balancer status: Backend server marked as “Healthy”. 03.06.25 11:40 Test with direct IP to Reverse Proxy → works. Test via FQDN → 502 Notable: Header “X-Forwarded-Proto” missing in external request. 04.06.25 09:05 Customer asks whether certificate may be incorrectly bound. Certificate validation: • CN correct • SAN correct • Chain complete No obvious errors. 05.06.25 08:50 Ticket forwarded to Network / Security Team. Assigned group changed to: BU Network & Security 06.06.25 09:30 Taken over by System Engineer 21 WAF analysis: • Policy active • TLS inspection active • HTTP/2 enabled Notable: After patch, default profile was reloaded. 06.06.25 11:15 Test: TLS inspection temporarily disabled → Login works. Inspection re-enabled → 502 again. 09.06.25 10:05 Further analysis: App server expects header: X-Forwarded-Proto: https When TLS inspection is active, header is not forwarded. 09.06.25 14:40 Comparison with architecture documentation performed. Reference in internal document: “Blueprint: Secure Web Publishing – KF40 – Version 3.2” Section 4.3 describes special header forwarding in case of TLS offloading. 10.06.25 08:25 Blueprint document loaded from DMS. Relevant notes: • TLS termination must occur on WAF • Backend expects HTTPS signaling • Custom header rewrite rule required • Inspection policy must not remove HTTP headers • Specific exception defined for portal application 10.06.25 11:45 Configuration comparison with blueprint shows: • Custom header rewrite rule missing • HTTP profile does not match documented version • Inspection template “Default-Strict” active instead of “Portal-App-Template” 12.06.25 Internal coordination: Firewall patch overwrote template. Custom policy was not reassigned. 16.06.25 Configuration reviewed and adjusted according to blueprint. No technical details documented in ticket (security policy). 18.06.25 14:55 External access test successful. Multiple login attempts tested. Monitoring stable for 24 hours. Ticket closure 18.06.25 15:33 Dear Contact Person 19 The root cause was a deviation in the Reverse Proxy configuration from the documented architectural specification following a security patch. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal blueprint documentation. The customer portal is now fully accessible again. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company SOURCE 17: ﻿ general info & description This document describes the blueprint for the secure publication of web-based applications of Customer Company 40 over the Internet. The architecture is based on a Reverse Proxy and Web Application Firewall (WAF) concept with TLS termination at the perimeter level. The objective is to protect internal application servers from direct Internet exposure while ensuring secure and controlled communication with external users. The Web Publishing architecture is used for the following applications: • Customer portal (portal.kf40.ch) • Partner access • Internal web services with external interface Central principles: • No direct publication of internal servers • TLS termination exclusively on the WAF • Backend servers are located in a separate server VLAN • HTTPS semantics must be preserved for the backend • Standardized templates for all portal applications Best practices: • Use of dedicated WAF templates per application • Documented header rewrite rules • No use of generic “Default-Strict” profiles for productive portals • Validation against this blueprint after every security patch MUST BE OBSERVED: All changes to WAF profiles, TLS policies, or Reverse Proxy rules must be validated against this document. design The architecture consists of three logical zones: Internet → Perimeter Zone (WAF / Reverse Proxy) → Internal Application Zone Components 1. Web Application Firewall (WAF) o TLS termination o Inspection policy o Header rewrite o Load balancing 2. Reverse Proxy Layer o Forwarding to application servers o Host header handling o Session persistence 3. Backend Application Server o Expects HTTPS semantics o No direct Internet access o Internal authentication TLS Offloading TLS is terminated on the WAF. Communication between the WAF and backend is performed over HTTP by default. In order for the backend to correctly interpret the original HTTPS request, the following headers must be set: • X-Forwarded-Proto: https • X-Forwarded-Host These headers must not be removed or overwritten by inspection or security profiles. WAF Template For portal applications, the template: Portal-App-Template must be used. This template contains: • Header rewrite rules • HTTPS semantics preservation • HTTP/2 compatibility configuration • Session handling • Adjustments for authentication flows The use of other templates (e.g., Default-Strict) is not permitted for productive portal services. After Security Patches After firmware or security updates, it is mandatory to verify: • Whether the correct template is still active • Whether custom rewrite rules remain in place • Whether inspection policies have not been reset • Whether TLS profiles remain unchanged decision tree & use cases 1. Question: Is the application reachable internally but not externally? Yes → Proceed to question 2. No → The issue is likely not within the Web Publishing layer. 2. Question: Is a 502 or 503 error displayed during external access? Yes → Check WAF template and header rewrite rules. No → Proceed to question 3. 3. Question: Is there a redirect loop between HTTP and HTTPS? Yes → Check X-Forwarded-Proto header. No → Proceed to question 4. 4. Question: Was a certificate recently renewed or a security patch applied? Yes → Validate configuration according to section “After Security Patches”. No → Proceed to question 5. 5. Question: Is TLS inspection enabled? Yes → Verify that the Portal-App-Template is used. No → Check backend health status.

The architecture is based on a Reverse Proxy and Web Application Firewall (WAF) concept with TLS termination at the perimeter level. The objective is to protect internal application servers from direct Internet exposure while ensuring secure and controlled communication with external users. The Web Publishing architecture is used for the following applications: • Customer portal (portal.kf40.ch) • Partner access • Internal web services with external interface Central principles: • No direct publication of internal servers • TLS termination exclusively on the WAF • Backend servers are located in a separate server VLAN • HTTPS semantics must be preserved for the backend • Standardized templates for all portal applications Best practices: • Use of dedicated WAF templates per application • Documented header rewrite rules • No use of generic “Default-Strict” profiles for productive portals • Validation against this blueprint after every security patch MUST BE OBSERVED: All changes to WAF profiles, TLS policies, or Reverse Proxy rules must be validated against this document. design The architecture consists of three logical zones: Internet → Perimeter Zone (WAF / Reverse Proxy) → Internal Application Zone Components 1. Web Application Firewall (WAF) o TLS termination o Inspection policy o Header rewrite o Load balancing 2. Reverse Proxy Layer o Forwarding to application servers o Host header handling o Session persistence 3. Backend Application Server o Expects HTTPS semantics o No direct Internet access o Internal authentication TLS Offloading TLS is terminated on the WAF. Communication between the WAF and backend is performed over HTTP by default. In order for the backend to correctly interpret the original HTTPS request, the following headers must be set: • X-Forwarded-Proto: https • X-Forwarded-Host These headers must not be removed or overwritten by inspection or security profiles. WAF Template For portal applications, the template: Portal-App-Template must be used. This template contains: • Header rewrite rules • HTTPS semantics preservation • HTTP/2 compatibility configuration • Session handling • Adjustments for authentication flows The use of other templates (e.g., Default-Strict) is not permitted for productive portal services. After Security Patches After firmware or security updates, it is mandatory to verify: • Whether the correct template is still active • Whether custom rewrite rules remain in place • Whether inspection policies have not been reset • Whether TLS profiles remain unchanged decision tree & use cases 1. Question: Is the application reachable internally but not externally? Yes → Proceed to question 2. No → The issue is likely not within the Web Publishing layer. 2. Question: Is a 502 or 503 error displayed during external access? Yes → Check WAF template and header rewrite rules. No → Proceed to question 3. 3. Question: Is there a redirect loop between HTTP and HTTPS? Yes → Check X-Forwarded-Proto header. No → Proceed to question 4. 4. Question: Was a certificate recently renewed or a security patch applied? Yes → Validate configuration according to section “After Security Patches”. No → Proceed to question 5. 5. Question: Is TLS inspection enabled? Yes → Verify that the Portal-App-Template is used. No → Check backend health status.

context: SOURCE 15: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF40: Customer Portal – Login via external URL not possible Ticket number: TI-1594408 Company: Customer Company 40 AG Contact person: Contact Person 19 Owner: Employee 29 Origin: Phone Type: Incident Created on: 03.06.25 07:52 Closure Closure date: 18.06.25 15:33 Closure text: Analysis completed. Configuration adjusted according to architectural specifications. Function restored. Activities 03.06.25 07:45 Phone report. Issue: External users can no longer log in to the customer portal. URL: https://portal.kf40.ch Browser error message: 502 – Bad Gateway Internally (via internal network) login works. Priority: High (customers affected) 03.06.25 07:52 Ticket created Assigned to: Employee 29 Status: In Progress Notes 03.06.25 08:30 Helpdesk checks: • External DNS resolution correct • Certificate valid • HTTPS reachable • Internal backend (app server) reachable Test: External → 502 Internal → works Suspicion: Reverse Proxy / WAF 03.06.25 09:10 Customer adds: A new certificate was installed over the weekend. Additionally, there was a firewall update (security patch). 03.06.25 10:25 Helpdesk checks firewall: • Rule for port 443 present • NAT correct • No blocking visible in logs Load balancer status: Backend server marked as “Healthy”. 03.06.25 11:40 Test with direct IP to Reverse Proxy → works. Test via FQDN → 502 Notable: Header “X-Forwarded-Proto” missing in external request. 04.06.25 09:05 Customer asks whether certificate may be incorrectly bound. Certificate validation: • CN correct • SAN correct • Chain complete No obvious errors. 05.06.25 08:50 Ticket forwarded to Network / Security Team. Assigned group changed to: BU Network & Security 06.06.25 09:30 Taken over by System Engineer 21 WAF analysis: • Policy active • TLS inspection active • HTTP/2 enabled Notable: After patch, default profile was reloaded. 06.06.25 11:15 Test: TLS inspection temporarily disabled → Login works. Inspection re-enabled → 502 again. 09.06.25 10:05 Further analysis: App server expects header: X-Forwarded-Proto: https When TLS inspection is active, header is not forwarded. 09.06.25 14:40 Comparison with architecture documentation performed. Reference in internal document: “Blueprint: Secure Web Publishing – KF40 – Version 3.2” Section 4.3 describes special header forwarding in case of TLS offloading. 10.06.25 08:25 Blueprint document loaded from DMS. Relevant notes: • TLS termination must occur on WAF • Backend expects HTTPS signaling • Custom header rewrite rule required • Inspection policy must not remove HTTP headers • Specific exception defined for portal application 10.06.25 11:45 Configuration comparison with blueprint shows: • Custom header rewrite rule missing • HTTP profile does not match documented version • Inspection template “Default-Strict” active instead of “Portal-App-Template” 12.06.25 Internal coordination: Firewall patch overwrote template. Custom policy was not reassigned. 16.06.25 Configuration reviewed and adjusted according to blueprint. No technical details documented in ticket (security policy). 18.06.25 14:55 External access test successful. Multiple login attempts tested. Monitoring stable for 24 hours. Ticket closure 18.06.25 15:33 Dear Contact Person 19 The root cause was a deviation in the Reverse Proxy configuration from the documented architectural specification following a security patch. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal blueprint documentation. The customer portal is now fully accessible again. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company
